title: PTC ThingWorx MCQ Quiz Bank – Set 2
description: Second set of 60 MCQs for a web app: 20 basic, 20 intermediate, 20 advanced. All questions are different from Set 1.

questions.question: Which best describes ThingWorx Composer from a beginner’s perspective?
options: A modeling environment where you define Things, services, and Mashups
A command-line tool for deploying edge devices
questions.answerText: A modeling environment where you define Things, services, and Mashups
questions.explanation: ThingWorx Composer is the main design-time tool used to model entities, define services, and configure Mashups on the platform.[web:2]

questions.question: In a beginner ThingWorx app, where do you typically bind Thing properties to widgets for display?
options: In the Mashup Builder data and widget binding panels
In the platform’s persistence provider settings
questions.answerText: In the Mashup Builder data and widget binding panels
questions.explanation: Mashup Builder lets you bind Thing properties and service outputs to widgets to create interactive screens.[web:14]

questions.question: Which entity is commonly used to group common configuration such as connection details that multiple Things share?
questions.answerText: Thing Template
questions.explanation: A Thing Template can centralize shared configuration and behavior that all derived Things use.[web:23]

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a Thing Shape?
options: To store historical data for Things
To provide reusable sets of properties and services that different Templates or Things can implement
questions.answerText: To provide reusable sets of properties and services that different Templates or Things can implement
questions.explanation: Thing Shapes hold shared capabilities that can be implemented by multiple Thing Templates and Things.[web:23][web:26]

questions.question: Which best describes an INFOTABLE in ThingWorx?
options: A tabular data structure often described by a Data Shape
questions.answerText: A tabular data structure often described by a Data Shape
questions.explanation: INFOTABLE is the standard type for tabular data, typically defined by a Data Shape.[web:24]

questions.question: What does a Value Stream automatically log when configured on a Thing?
options: Property value changes that are marked as logged
questions.answerText: Property value changes that are marked as logged
questions.explanation: When a property on a Thing is set to logged and linked to a Value Stream, its changes are automatically recorded.[web:19]

questions.question: Which entity would you use to store files such as documents, images, or CSV exports in ThingWorx?
questions.answerText: File Repository Thing
questions.explanation: File Repository Things provide access to files stored on the platform’s file system for upload and download.[web:8]

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Organizations entity in ThingWorx?
options: To model hierarchical relationships such as business units for visibility and security
questions.answerText: To model hierarchical relationships such as business units for visibility and security
questions.explanation: Organizations model hierarchies (like regions or departments) that can be used for entity visibility and access control.[web:8]

questions.question: Which entity is primarily responsible for defining what a logged-in user can or cannot do in ThingWorx?
questions.answerText: User and User Group permissions
questions.explanation: Users and user groups have permissions and visibility settings that control their access to entities and services.[web:8]

questions.question: What is a simple way to test a service you created on a Thing in Composer?
options: Only by calling it from an Edge device
By running it from the Services tab’s Test area in Composer
questions.answerText: By running it from the Services tab’s Test area in Composer
questions.explanation: Composer provides a Test area on the Services tab where you can execute services and see their results.[web:2]

questions.question: You want a simple list showing all connected devices and whether each is online or offline. Which basic design is most appropriate?
options: A Mashup with a Grid widget bound to a Thing service returning an INFOTABLE of device status
A Value Stream with numeric aggregation
A File Repository with one file per Thing
questions.answerText: A Mashup with a Grid widget bound to a Thing service returning an INFOTABLE of device status
questions.explanation: A service returning an INFOTABLE of devices can be bound to a Grid widget in a Mashup to display their states.[web:14]

questions.question: Your operator Mashup should allow the user to manually set a Boolean property 'RemoteEnabled' on a pump Thing. What is a straightforward approach?
options: Bind a Button widget’s click event to a service that sets the property
Edit the property only in Composer
Use a Value Stream to configure the property
Use a Stream Thing for configuration
questions.answerText: Bind a Button widget’s click event to a service that sets the property
questions.explanation: You typically expose a service that updates the property and bind a button click or toggle widget to that service in the Mashup.[web:14]

questions.question: You are building a basic dashboard for energy meters with numeric values and trend charts. Which two Widget types are most likely to be used?
options: Property Display and Time Series Chart widgets
File Upload and Text Area widgets
questions.answerText: Property Display and Time Series Chart widgets
questions.explanation: Numeric and historical data are typically shown using property/value display and time-series chart widgets on a Mashup.[web:14]

questions.question: A simple manufacturing KPI screen should update every 10 seconds. How can you trigger periodic updates without user interaction?
options: Using a Timer or Auto-Refresh widget that repeatedly invokes a data service
Manually pressing a button each time
questions.answerText: Using a Timer or Auto-Refresh widget that repeatedly invokes a data service
questions.explanation: Timer-style widgets can periodically call services to refresh data on a Mashup.[web:14]

questions.question: A beginner developer wants to expose a total production count from several Machines. Which approach is the most straightforward?
options: Create one Thing with one property per Machine and no services
Write a service that sums properties from several Machine Things and return an INFOTABLE or number
Create a separate Mashup per Machine only
Use only Organizations to aggregate data
questions.answerText: Write a service that sums properties from several Machine Things and return an INFOTABLE or number
questions.explanation: Aggregating values is commonly done with a server-side service that reads properties from multiple Things and returns a computed result.[web:2]

questions.question: You want to quickly prototype an IoT application to monitor a handful of sensors without writing much custom code. Which feature of ThingWorx supports this?
options: Low-code modeling with Things and Mashups
questions.answerText: Low-code modeling with Things and Mashups
questions.explanation: ThingWorx provides a low-code, model-driven environment that lets you create IoT applications largely through configuration.[web:2]

questions.question: At a basic level, what does ThingWorx Kepware Server provide to a ThingWorx solution?
options: Industrial protocol connectivity between PLCs/devices and ThingWorx
questions.answerText: Industrial protocol connectivity between PLCs/devices and ThingWorx
questions.explanation: Kepware acts as an industrial connectivity layer that communicates with PLCs and devices using protocols like OPC and exposes data to ThingWorx.[web:34][web:36]

questions.question: Which is a common use of ThingWorx Flow in simple integrations?
options: Orchestrating workflows that call external systems like SAP or email
questions.answerText: Orchestrating workflows that call external systems like SAP or email
questions.explanation: ThingWorx Flow is used to build workflows that integrate ThingWorx data and events with external systems via connectors.[web:3]

questions.question: At a beginner level, what is the main purpose of an AWS IoT or AWS connector in a ThingWorx solution?
options: To move IoT data between ThingWorx and AWS services
questions.answerText: To move IoT data between ThingWorx and AWS services
questions.explanation: Connectors allow ThingWorx to send data to and receive data from AWS for analytics and other cloud workloads.[web:32]

questions.question: Which statement about connectivity is true for a basic ThingWorx architecture?
options: ThingWorx only accepts manual CSV uploads
Connectivity can be achieved both via direct device SDKs and via gateways like Kepware
Only devices that run Composer can connect
Connectivity is limited to HTTP POST requests
questions.answerText: Connectivity can be achieved both via direct device SDKs and via gateways like Kepware
questions.explanation: Devices can connect using ThingWorx Edge SDKs or through connectivity products such as Kepware that bridge industrial protocols to ThingWorx.[web:2][web:34]

questions.question: Which type of property is most appropriate for representing a temperature that comes from an OPC-connected sensor and changes frequently?
options: A persistent, logged numeric property on the Thing
questions.answerText: A persistent, logged numeric property on the Thing
questions.explanation: Frequently changing sensor values are typically modeled as numeric properties that can be persisted and logged in a Value Stream.[web:19]

questions.question: Which statement about Tags in ThingWorx is correct?
options: Tags are only used for file uploads
Tags allow you to categorize entities to simplify search and organization
Tags can only be applied to Mashups
questions.answerText: Tags allow you to categorize entities to simplify search and organization
questions.explanation: Model tags are metadata that help group and search entities by functional, organizational, or other criteria.[web:8]

questions.question: In an intermediate app, why might you implement common alarm logic in a Thing Shape rather than repeating it on each Thing?
options: To centralize and reuse alarm logic across many asset Things
Because Shapes are only for UI
questions.answerText: To centralize and reuse alarm logic across many asset Things
questions.explanation: Placing alarm logic in a Shape that many Things implement improves reuse and maintainability.[web:23][web:26]

questions.question: Which statement about runtime permissions is accurate?
options: Runtime permissions control who can execute services and read properties
Runtime permissions only apply to Mashups
Runtime permissions are set globally and cannot vary per entity
questions.answerText: Runtime permissions control who can execute services and read properties
questions.explanation: ThingWorx supports design-time and runtime permissions; runtime governs access to services and data at run time.[web:8]

questions.question: Why is it recommended to avoid defining many custom properties directly on a Thing when a Thing Template exists?
options: It breaks reuse and makes it harder to propagate changes across all assets
Properties must always be defined on Mashups
questions.answerText: It breaks reuse and makes it harder to propagate changes across all assets
questions.explanation: Defining shared properties on the Template or Shape ensures consistency as assets scale.[web:26]

questions.question: For moderate volumes of event entries from multiple Things with different schemas, which storage pattern is typically more suitable?
options: A shared Stream for generic events
questions.answerText: A shared Stream for generic events
questions.explanation: Streams are suited to heterogeneous, event-style entries coming from multiple Things.[web:19][web:22]

questions.question: Which built-in mechanism helps you record when a Thing property crosses a particular limit, in addition to Events?
questions.answerText: Alert definitions on properties
questions.explanation: Property alerts let you declare thresholds that trigger alert states and can be tied to further logic.[web:5]

questions.question: When querying a Value Stream for a property history, which type of result is typically returned?
questions.answerText: An INFOTABLE with time-stamped rows
questions.explanation: Value Stream queries return an INFOTABLE where each row contains a timestamp and value for the property.[web:19]

questions.question: Which is a good reason to use a Data Table instead of a Stream?
options: You need primary key–based lookups over relatively static reference data
You only have numeric sensor readings
questions.answerText: You need primary key–based lookups over relatively static reference data
questions.explanation: Data Tables behave like keyed tables and are best for configuration or reference records.[web:22][web:24]

questions.question: What is a common use of Resources in ThingWorx?
options: To provide shared utility services and code libraries across the model
questions.answerText: To provide shared utility services and code libraries across the model
questions.explanation: Resource entities are often used to host reusable services and helper functions.[web:8]

questions.question: You are designing a plant overview Mashup that loads slowly because many services fire at once. Which optimization is a reasonable next step?
options: Call all services from a single master service and bind the result
Increase the polling rate for every service
questions.answerText: Call all services from a single master service and bind the result
questions.explanation: Aggregating data retrieval into a single service reduces client–server round-trips and simplifies bindings.[web:8]

questions.question: You have a Mashup for operators and another for supervisors; both show machine lists but with different detail. How could you avoid duplicating list logic?
options: Place the list in a Thing Shape Mashup that both screens embed
Copy and paste widgets between Mashups
Put everything into one giant Mashup
questions.answerText: Place the list in a Thing Shape Mashup that both screens embed
questions.explanation: Reusable Mashup components allow different layouts to share the same core list logic and bindings.[web:14]

questions.question: A customer wants to filter a list of machines by site and by line, with results updating without full-page reload. Which pattern fits?
options: Using Mashup parameters bound to Dropdown widgets that pass filters to a data service
Storing filters in a file repository
questions.answerText: Using Mashup parameters bound to Dropdown widgets that pass filters to a data service
questions.explanation: Filtering is typically implemented by binding widget selections into service inputs via Mashup parameters.[web:14]

questions.question: You need to expose a KPI summary service from a central 'PlantKPIs' Thing to several Mashups. What is a good security approach?
options: Set appropriate runtime execute permissions on the KPI service and use visibility based on Organizations
questions.answerText: Set appropriate runtime execute permissions on the KPI service and use visibility based on Organizations
questions.explanation: Intermediate apps often use organization-based visibility and specific runtime permissions to secure shared services.[web:8]

questions.question: Your Value Stream contains millions of historical rows. A Mashup showing a full history chart is slow. What is a sensible optimization?
options: Always request the entire history range
Add time range inputs in the Mashup and query only the needed interval
questions.answerText: Add time range inputs in the Mashup and query only the needed interval
questions.explanation: Limiting queries to relevant time windows reduces data transferred and speeds up charts.[web:19]

questions.question: An engineering team wants to simulate sensor data on a development server without connecting real devices. How can they do this?
options: Use services or timers on a SimulatedDevice Thing to periodically update properties
questions.answerText: Use services or timers on a SimulatedDevice Thing to periodically update properties
questions.explanation: Simulated device Things using scheduled services or timers are a common pattern for dev/test environments.[web:2]

questions.question: In an intermediate integration, why might you use ThingWorx Flow instead of writing custom JavaScript services for each external call?
options: Flow provides visual orchestration and connectors that simplify calling many external APIs
questions.answerText: Flow provides visual orchestration and connectors that simplify calling many external APIs
questions.explanation: Flow offers connector-based, low-code integration with systems such as SAP, email, and messaging platforms.[web:3]

questions.question: You must integrate with a legacy OPC DA server and a new MQTT-based sensor network. Which connectivity tool can help bridge both into ThingWorx?
options: ThingWorx Kepware Server or Kepware Edge with appropriate drivers and MQTT interfaces
questions.answerText: ThingWorx Kepware Server or Kepware Edge with appropriate drivers and MQTT interfaces
questions.explanation: Kepware products can talk OPC DA, OPC UA, and MQTT, exposing data to ThingWorx through standard interfaces.[web:34][web:36]

questions.question: An SAP team exposes an OData service with production order data. How can ThingWorx consume it without custom HTTP code in every Mashup?
options: Use a ThingWorx Flow SAP OData connector and expose its services to Mashups
questions.answerText: Use a ThingWorx Flow SAP OData connector and expose its services to Mashups
questions.explanation: The SAP OData connector in Flow provides reusable services that can be bound to Mashups.[web:3]

questions.question: Why might an intermediate solution choose to route some device data first to a cloud IoT hub (like AWS IoT) before ThingWorx?
options: To leverage cloud-side rules, analytics, and scaling before forwarding curated data to ThingWorx
questions.answerText: To leverage cloud-side rules, analytics, and scaling before forwarding curated data to ThingWorx
questions.explanation: Cloud IoT hubs can handle ingestion and analytics at scale, and connectors can forward results into ThingWorx.[web:32]

questions.question: In a large-scale deployment, why is it recommended to factor shared behavior into multiple small Thing Shapes instead of one huge Shape?
options: To improve composability, testability, and reduce unintended coupling between unrelated capabilities
Because Templates cannot implement more than one Shape
questions.answerText: To improve composability, testability, and reduce unintended coupling between unrelated capabilities
questions.explanation: Smaller, focused Shapes keep the model modular and easier to maintain as the solution grows.[web:23][web:26]

questions.question: Which advanced modeling choice most directly affects the ability to extend an asset type over time without recreating all Instances?
options: Selection of the base Thing Template and implemented Shapes
questions.answerText: Selection of the base Thing Template and implemented Shapes
questions.explanation: Because the base Template cannot change later, choosing the right Template and Shape composition is critical for extensibility.[web:26]

questions.question: How can you encapsulate complex validation logic for a group of related properties so it can be reused across multiple asset types?
options: Create a shared validation service on a Resource or Shape and call it from asset services
Hard-code validation inside each Mashup widget
Store validation rules in a file repository
questions.answerText: Create a shared validation service on a Resource or Shape and call it from asset services
questions.explanation: Placing validation in reusable server-side services prevents duplication and ensures consistent enforcement.[web:8][web:23]

questions.question: For high-frequency telemetry where each asset has multiple numeric signals, which combination is typically best for long-term history and query performance?
options: One Stream per signal with text payloads
A Value Stream per asset for signal properties, with careful indexing and retention settings
Storing only in Mashup session variables
One Data Table row per sample
questions.answerText: A Value Stream per asset for signal properties, with careful indexing and retention settings
questions.explanation: Value Streams are optimized for time-series property history; per-asset design and retention improves scalability.[web:19][web:22]

questions.question: Which advanced Data Shape design helps support schema evolution in a production system?
options: Never changing Data Shapes and recreating all data tables instead
Using optional fields and version indicators to allow new fields without breaking existing consumers
Deleting all unused fields during runtime
Using only primitive types without metadata
questions.answerText: Using optional fields and version indicators to allow new fields without breaking existing consumers
questions.explanation: Designing Data Shapes with backward-compatible changes reduces impact when adding new columns.[web:24]

questions.question: In a multi-tenant ThingWorx solution, what is a common pattern for isolating data and access while keeping a shared model?
options: Running a separate platform instance per tenant only
Using tenant-aware Organizations, runtime visibility rules, and per-tenant asset instances that share Templates and Shapes
questions.answerText: Using tenant-aware Organizations, runtime visibility rules, and per-tenant asset instances that share Templates and Shapes
questions.explanation: A shared meta-model with tenant-specific instances governed by Organizations and permissions is a typical pattern.[web:8][web:26]

questions.question: Which advanced security approach helps ensure that composable services cannot be misused by other developers in a large team?
options: Using fine-grained runtime execute permissions at the service level and grouping them via Collections
questions.answerText: Using fine-grained runtime execute permissions at the service level and grouping them via Collections
questions.explanation: Collections and fine-grained execute permissions help control who can invoke particular services.[web:8]

questions.question: In terms of digital twin fidelity, which practice improves the usefulness of your Thing models for operations teams?
options: Keeping only a single aggregated status field
Modeling key operational states, KPIs, and relationships (such as line–machine–sensor hierarchies) explicitly in the Thing model
questions.answerText: Modeling key operational states, KPIs, and relationships (such as line–machine–sensor hierarchies) explicitly in the Thing model
questions.explanation: Rich, structured digital twins reflect operational reality and support better monitoring and analysis.[web:2][web:33]

questions.question: Which approach best leverages the separation between modeling and visualization layers in ThingWorx?
options: Embedding all business logic directly in Mashup JavaScript
Keeping business logic in Thing and Resource services and using Mashups only for binding and interaction
questions.answerText: Keeping business logic in Thing and Resource services and using Mashups only for binding and interaction
questions.explanation: Placing business rules in server-side services ensures reusability and keeps UI concerns separate.[web:2][web:14]

questions.question: When designing a microservices-style architecture around ThingWorx, which pattern aligns best with scalable domain boundaries?
options: One monolithic Thing for all functionality
Multiple domain-specific Things and Resources with clear service interfaces and minimal cross-dependencies
Centralizing all logic in one Resource
questions.answerText: Multiple domain-specific Things and Resources with clear service interfaces and minimal cross-dependencies
questions.explanation: Domain-focused services and clear interfaces reduce coupling and align with microservice principles.[web:8][web:26]

questions.question: You need to roll out a new calculated KPI across thousands of machine Things without manual edits. The KPI uses existing signals already modeled on a Shape. What is a robust strategy?
options: Add the KPI property and calculation service to the shared Shape and deploy that Shape to all relevant Templates
Implement the KPI only in Mashup scripts
Create a separate Value Stream per KPI
questions.answerText: Add the KPI property and calculation service to the shared Shape and deploy that Shape to all relevant Templates
questions.explanation: Updating a shared Shape ensures that all implementing Templates and Things inherit the new KPI logic.[web:23][web:26]

questions.question: An enterprise wants to maintain strict separation between development, test, and production ThingWorx environments while reusing the same model. What approach helps?
options: Using export/import of entities, solution packages, and environment-specific configuration Things (for URLs, credentials, etc.)
Sharing the same database for all environments
questions.answerText: Using export/import of entities, solution packages, and environment-specific configuration Things (for URLs, credentials, etc.)
questions.explanation: Packaging and promoting model entities while externalizing environment-specific settings is a standard ALM pattern.[web:8]

questions.question: High-frequency telemetry from thousands of assets is now being persisted in a single Value Stream, and query performance is degrading. Which advanced refactor makes sense?
options: Switching all history to one Data Table
Partitioning history into multiple Value Streams by asset type or region and routing logging accordingly
questions.answerText: Partitioning history into multiple Value Streams by asset type or region and routing logging accordingly
questions.explanation: Partitioning Value Streams reduces contention and improves query performance at scale.[web:19][web:22]

questions.question: A global manufacturer requires that each region can manage its own assets and dashboards while a central team can see everything. Which design aligns with this requirement?
options: Creating one Organization per region, assigning user groups accordingly, and using visibility rules on Things and Mashups
Storing region names only in Data Tables
Duplicating the entire model per region on separate servers
questions.answerText: Creating one Organization per region, assigning user groups accordingly, and using visibility rules on Things and Mashups
questions.explanation: Organizations and visibility rules allow hierarchical access control with global oversight.[web:8]

questions.question: Your customer wants to plug in new analytics microservices (for anomaly detection) without changing every Mashup. What is a flexible approach?
options: Bind Mashups directly to each new microservice URL
Define an abstraction layer of ThingWorx services that call external analytics, and keep Mashups bound to these stable services
questions.answerText: Define an abstraction layer of ThingWorx services that call external analytics, and keep Mashups bound to these stable services
questions.explanation: An abstraction layer decouples UI from specific external analytics implementations.[web:8][web:33]

questions.question: Operations teams complain that an interactive dashboard sometimes shows stale data when new events arrive slightly out of order from multiple sources. Which design can address this?
options: Ignoring timestamps and using arrival order
Buffering updates in a Value Stream and rendering based on event timestamps instead of arrival times
questions.answerText: Buffering updates in a Value Stream and rendering based on event timestamps instead of arrival times
questions.explanation: Using timestamped entries in a Value Stream lets you reconstruct a consistent time-ordered view of events.[web:19][web:11]

questions.question: You want to ingest telemetry through AWS IoT Core and then forward selected data into ThingWorx for visualization. Which combination fits an advanced pattern?
options: AWS IoT rules to route messages to the ThingWorx AWS IoT Connector or HTTP endpoints, then mapping into Thing models
questions.answerText: AWS IoT rules to route messages to the ThingWorx AWS IoT Connector or HTTP endpoints, then mapping into Thing models
questions.explanation: AWS IoT rules can forward data to ThingWorx via connectors or APIs as part of an integrated architecture.[web:32]

questions.question: A customer wants to use Kepware for on-premise connectivity but also forward certain tags to a cloud analytics platform directly. How can ThingWorx fit into this design?
options: It cannot be used with Kepware
ThingWorx can consume Kepware tags for operational dashboards while Kepware also republishes selected data to cloud endpoints
ThingWorx must receive all traffic first
questions.answerText: ThingWorx can consume Kepware tags for operational dashboards while Kepware also republishes selected data to cloud endpoints
questions.explanation: Kepware can act as a hub, feeding ThingWorx for HMI while also streaming data to cloud services for analytics.[web:34][web:36]

questions.question: An SAP integration requires both synchronous queries (for dashboards) and asynchronous updates (for events). Which combination is appropriate?
options: OData-based synchronous services plus event-driven flows (for example, using ThingWorx Flow) to push updates
questions.answerText: OData-based synchronous services plus event-driven flows (for example, using ThingWorx Flow) to push updates
questions.explanation: Combining OData calls for queries with event-driven flows for updates meets both interaction patterns.[web:3][web:9]

questions.question: Why are extension-based connectors (for example, AWS, SAP, MQTT) preferable to ad‑hoc custom HTTP calls scattered across Mashups?
options: They remove the need for authentication
They provide centralized, testable integration points with supported configuration and upgrade paths
They force all logic into the UI
questions.answerText: They provide centralized, testable integration points with supported configuration and upgrade paths
questions.explanation: Using supported connectors concentrates integration logic in one place, simplifying maintenance and upgrades.[web:32][web:34][web:36]
